section.title: Paul and the False Apostles
section.sectionRationale: Paul's defense sharpens into open warning. Like a father who has betrothed his daughter to one husband, he is jealous with a godly jealousy to present the Corinthian church as a pure virgin to Christ, and he fears they are being seduced from their simple devotion to Him. He unmasks the intruders who preach a different Jesus as false apostles, deceitful workers, servants of the Satan who masquerades as an angel of light.
section.thesis: Godly jealousy longs to keep the church in pure and sincere devotion to Christ alone; those who preach another Jesus, a different spirit, and a different gospel are not superior apostles but false apostles and deceitful workers, servants of Satan disguised as servants of righteousness, whose end will match their deeds.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the church betrothed as a pure virgin to Christ and Paul's fear of a second Eve; his preaching free of charge over against the super-apostles; and the unmasking of the false apostles as Satan's servants in disguise.

units.label: Betrothed to one husband
units.note: as Eve was deceived, so your minds may be led astray

verses.text: I hope you will put up with a little of my foolishness, but you are already doing that.
I am jealous for you with a godly {{zelos|jealousy}}. For I promised you to one husband, to present you as a pure virgin to Christ.
I am afraid, however, that just as Eve was deceived by the serpent’s cunning, your minds may be led astray from your {{haplotes|simple and pure devotion}} to Christ.

For if someone comes and proclaims a Jesus other than the One we proclaimed, or if you receive a different spirit than the One you received, or a different gospel than the one you accepted, you put up with it very easily.

units.label: Preaching free of charge
units.note: in no way inferior to the super-apostles

verses.text: I consider myself in no way inferior to those “super-apostles.”
Although I am not a polished speaker, I am certainly not lacking in knowledge. We have made this clear to you in every way possible.
Was it a sin for me to humble myself in order to exalt you, because I preached the gospel of God to you free of charge?
I robbed other churches by accepting their support in order to serve you.

And when I was with you and in need, I was not a burden to anyone; for the brothers who came from Macedonia supplied my needs. I have refrained from being a burden to you in any way, and I will continue to do so.
As surely as the truth of Christ is in me, this boasting of mine will not be silenced in the regions of Achaia.
Why? Because I do not love you? God knows I do!

units.label: Masquerading as apostles
units.note: Satan disguised as an angel of light

verses.text: But I will keep on doing what I am doing, in order to undercut those who want an opportunity to be regarded as our equals in the things of which they boast.
For such men are {{pseudapostolos|false apostles}}, deceitful workers, masquerading as apostles of Christ.
And no wonder, for Satan himself masquerades as an angel of light.
It is not surprising, then, if his servants masquerade as servants of righteousness. Their end will correspond to their actions.

zelos.gloss: Zeal, ardor, or jealousy. The word can be a sin, envious rivalry, or a holy passion; Paul specifies a godly jealousy, a jealousy that belongs to God. Like a father guarding his betrothed daughter, Paul burns with the same jealous love that God has for His covenant people. It is not petty possessiveness but a righteous longing that the church be kept wholly for Christ, her one true husband.

haplotes.gloss: Simplicity, singleness, sincerity. It describes an undivided heart, a devotion to Christ that is pure and uncomplicated by rival loyalties. Paul fears the Corinthians will be lured away from this single-hearted attachment to Christ, just as Eve was drawn from trusting God's word. The word warns that the gospel can be lost not only by open denial but by the subtle diluting of wholehearted love for the Savior.

pseudapostolos.gloss: A false apostle, a counterfeit sent one. Paul coins a blunt term for the intruders who pose as apostles of Christ but serve another master. However impressive their claims, they are deceitful workers whose true allegiance is to Satan, who disguises himself as an angel of light. The word strips away the mask: they are not merely mistaken teachers but frauds whose end will match their deeds.

basic.context: Paul asks the Corinthians to bear with a little foolishness as he defends himself. He writes as a spiritual father who betrothed the church to one husband, Christ, and now fears she is being seduced away, as Eve was deceived by the serpent. Rival teachers, whom he sarcastically calls super-apostles, have won a hearing by preaching a different Jesus. Paul unmasks them as false apostles and servants of Satan disguised as servants of righteousness.
basic.hermeneutics: This is impassioned pastoral warning, not calm exposition. Read the marriage imagery of verses 2 to 3 as the key: the church is engaged to Christ, and any teaching that draws her from single-hearted devotion to Him is spiritual adultery. Paul's refusal to take pay from Corinth, verses 7 to 11, is part of the argument, distinguishing him from the money-motivated intruders.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul burns with zelos, a godly jealousy, to keep the church for Christ. He fears they will be led from their haplotes, their simple and sincere devotion to Christ, as Eve was deceived. And he names the intruders plainly as pseudapostolos, false apostles, counterfeits who only masquerade as apostles of Christ.
basic.typology: The betrothal of the church as a pure virgin to one husband fulfills the Old Testament picture of God as the husband of His people and looks ahead to the marriage of the Lamb. The deception of Eve becomes the pattern of every seduction from the truth, and the serpent behind it is unmasked as Satan still at work through false teachers.
basic.christ: Christ is the one husband to whom the church is betrothed, the pure and jealous love of the whole passage. He is the true Jesus whom the false teachers counterfeit with a different Jesus. The gospel Paul guards is the gospel of the real Christ, freely given, over against every disguised distortion that would rob the church of Him.
basic.god: God is the one whose jealousy Paul shares, the God who will not share His people's devotion with idols or impostors. He knows Paul's love, verse 11, and He stands behind the truth of Christ. The passage reveals a God who is at war with Satan's counterfeits and who will bring the deceivers to an end that matches their deeds.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 3 records the serpent's deception of Eve, which Paul makes his warning. Galatians 1 pronounces a curse on any different gospel. Ephesians 5 pictures Christ and the church as husband and bride. Revelation 19 shows the marriage of the Lamb to His pure bride.
basic.oneStory: Scripture tells of God betrothing a people to Himself and guarding them against the serpent's lies. Paul stands in that story as the friend of the Bridegroom, jealous to present the church as a pure virgin to Christ. The counterfeit gospel is the serpent's old work in new disguise, and the story runs toward the day when the true Bridegroom receives His spotless bride.

context.literary: Paul continues the fool's boast begun in chapter 10, now with vivid marriage imagery and biting sarcasm about the super-apostles. Verses 1 to 4 set the theme of seduction from Christ; verses 5 to 11 defend Paul's practice of preaching free of charge; verses 12 to 15 tear the mask off the intruders. The unit prepares for the extended boasting in weakness that follows.
context.historical: Traveling teachers in the Greco-Roman world commonly charged fees and sought patrons, and a speaker who took no pay could be suspected of having nothing worth paying for. Paul supported himself and received help from Macedonia precisely so the Corinthians would owe him nothing, distinguishing his motives from the rivals who exploited the church for gain and status.
context.canonical: The passage joins Galatians 1 as a fierce defense of the one true gospel against counterfeits. Its unmasking of Satan as an angel of light shaped the church's understanding that false teaching often comes dressed in piety, and its bridal imagery stands with Ephesians 5 and Revelation 19 in the Bible's marriage theme.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To warn the Corinthians, out of a jealous, fatherly love, that they are being seduced from pure devotion to Christ by teachers who preach a different Jesus, and to expose these intruders as false apostles and servants of Satan rather than the superior apostles they claim to be.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Paul describes a specific crisis at Corinth and his own conduct within it. Yet the enduring truths are prescriptive: the church owes single-hearted devotion to Christ, a different gospel must be rejected, and false teaching often wears the disguise of righteousness and must be discerned.

debates.question: Are the super-apostles the same people as the false apostles?

views.label: The same group, named with irony
views.case: Read straight through, the super-apostles of verse 5 are the intruders unmasked as false apostles in verse 13; the sarcastic title and the blunt term describe one set of opponents at Corinth.

views.label: Two distinct groups
views.case: Some argue the super-apostles are the respected Jerusalem apostles, to whom Paul claims equality, while the false apostles are the local intruders who appeal to them; the language and tone differ.

debates.bearing: Most read them as the same opponents, since the whole passage targets one threat; the historic reading takes super-apostles as Paul's ironic name for the very men he then exposes as counterfeits.

debates.question: What is the different Jesus, spirit, and gospel of verse 4?

views.label: A real distortion of the true gospel
views.case: The intruders preach a Christ and message so altered that to accept them is to abandon the gospel Paul preached; the contrast with a pure devotion to Christ makes the danger genuine.

views.label: Chiefly a rhetorical exaggeration
views.case: Some suggest Paul overstates to shock the Corinthians, and the real dispute is more about style, credentials, and money than doctrine.

debates.bearing: The marriage imagery and the charge of serving Satan show Paul regards the threat as a real seduction from Christ, not a mere quarrel over style; the counterfeit gospel genuinely endangers the church.

study.originalLanguages: Paul writes as a jealous father. His zelos is a godly jealousy, the very jealousy of God for His covenant people, longing that the church be kept a pure virgin for Christ her one husband. He dreads that their haplotes, their single-hearted, sincere devotion to Christ, will be corrupted as Eve was deceived by the serpent's cunning. Then he coins a stark word for the intruders: pseudapostolos, false apostles, counterfeit sent ones who masquerade as apostles of Christ while serving the Satan who himself masquerades as an angel of light.

typology.type: The church betrothed as a pure virgin to one husband
typology.fulfillment: Fulfills God as the husband of His people and anticipates the marriage of the Lamb to His spotless bride in the new creation.

typology.type: The deception of Eve by the serpent
typology.fulfillment: Becomes the pattern of every seduction from the truth; the serpent is unmasked as Satan, still deceiving through false apostles disguised as servants of righteousness.

study.christ: Christ is the one husband to whom the church is engaged, the pure object of the jealous love that drives the whole passage. He is the true Jesus whom the intruders counterfeit with a different Jesus, and the gospel Paul preaches free of charge is the good news of this real Christ. Every distortion of Him is spiritual adultery against the Bridegroom. The passage guards the church for the day when Christ presents her to Himself without spot or blemish.
study.god: God is jealous for His people, and Paul shares that jealousy as the friend of the Bridegroom. God knows Paul's heart and love for the Corinthians, verse 11, and He stands behind the truth of Christ that Paul defends. He is at war with the counterfeits of Satan, and He will bring the deceivers to an end that matches their deeds, so that His pure gospel and His pure bride are preserved.

crossReferences.note: The serpent deceives Eve by cunning, the pattern of the seduction Paul fears at Corinth.
A curse on anyone who preaches a different gospel than the one received.
Christ loves the church and will present her to Himself as a pure bride.
The marriage of the Lamb, when the pure bride is finally presented to Christ.

study.oneStory: The Bible is in part the story of God betrothing a people to Himself and guarding them from the serpent's lies. Paul stands in that story as the friend of the Bridegroom, jealous to present the Corinthian church as a pure virgin to Christ. The counterfeit gospel of the false apostles is the old work of the serpent in new disguise, and the story presses on to the marriage of the Lamb, when the true Bridegroom receives His spotless bride.

quiz.q: Why is Paul jealous for the Corinthians with a godly jealousy?

options.text: Because he betrothed them to one husband, Christ, and wants to present them pure to Him.
options.note: Verse 2 gives the marriage imagery behind Paul's jealousy.

options.text: Because he wants them loyal to himself rather than other teachers.
options.note: His jealousy is for Christ, not for his own following.

options.text: Because he envies the success of the other apostles.
options.note: This is a godly jealousy for the church, not sinful envy.
Money is not the issue in his jealousy; devotion to Christ is.

quiz.q: What does Paul fear will happen to the Corinthians, and by what example?

options.text: That their minds will be led astray from pure devotion to Christ, as Eve was deceived.
options.note: Verse 3 compares the danger to the serpent's deception of Eve.

options.text: That they will simply grow bored with the gospel over time.
options.note: The danger is active seduction by deceivers, not mere boredom.

options.text: That they will face persecution and give up.
options.note: The threat here is deception, not persecution.

options.text: That they will become too generous with their money.
options.note: The concern is spiritual seduction from Christ, not generosity.

quiz.q: Why did Paul preach the gospel to the Corinthians free of charge?

options.text: To humble himself and exalt them, being no burden, unlike the money-seeking rivals.
options.note: Verses 7 to 9 show Paul refusing support to serve them freely.

options.text: Because the gospel has no real value worth paying for.
options.note: He preached free of charge out of love, not because the gospel lacked worth.

options.text: Because he was independently wealthy and needed nothing.
options.note: He was in need, but Macedonian brothers supplied him.

options.text: Because he was forbidden to accept any money at all.
options.note: He did accept support from other churches; this was his choice for Corinth.

quiz.q: How does Paul unmask the false apostles in verses 13 to 15?

options.text: As deceitful workers and servants of Satan, who masquerades as an angel of light.
options.note: Paul exposes their true master behind the disguise of righteousness.

options.text: As sincere but poorly trained teachers.
options.note: Paul calls them frauds, not merely undertrained.

options.text: As rivals who simply have a different style.
options.note: The issue is not style but a counterfeit gospel and false allegiance.
Paul warns they are dangerous servants of Satan whose end matches their deeds.

questions: Paul is jealous to present the church as a pure virgin to Christ. What would single-hearted, undivided devotion to Christ look like in your own life right now?
Paul fears the Corinthians will be deceived as Eve was. What subtle teachings or cultural pressures are drawing your heart from simple devotion to Christ?
A different Jesus, a different spirit, a different gospel can be received very easily. How do you test what you hear against the true gospel Paul preached?
Paul refused pay so he would be no burden and his motives would be clear. Where might your own generosity or restraint make the gospel more believable to others?
Satan disguises himself as an angel of light, and false teaching often looks righteous. How can you grow in discernment without becoming cynical or suspicious of everyone?

translationNotes.note: Godly jealousy translates a phrase meaning the jealousy of God; the word zelos can be sinful envy or, as here, a holy, protective ardor for the church's fidelity to Christ.
Simple and pure devotion renders haplotes, singleness or sincerity of heart; some manuscripts add and purity, but the sense is an undivided attachment to Christ.
Super-apostles is Paul's ironic title for his opponents, kept in curly quotation marks as in the BSB to signal the sarcasm.
False apostles renders pseudapostolos, a term Paul coins for counterfeit sent ones; masquerading translates a word for disguising or transforming oneself in outward appearance.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 2 Corinthians 11:1-15. Footnote markers stripped. No em or en dashes occurred in this passage, so no dash substitution was required. Curly quotation marks retained as in the BSB, including around super-apostles.
